content.text: When a human resumes a paused agent, they often want to do more than just continue — they want to correct the agent's direction, fix a bad tool result, prune items from the work queue, or revoke a session-level approval. Editing agent state lets operators intervene precisely without discarding all the work done so far.

attrs.alt: Agent state components: editable vs dangerous-to-modify

content.text: Not all state components are safe to modify. The key distinction is whether the change affects the model's view of what has already happened:
Not part of conversation history; just a task list
Controls future approvals, not past ones
Used for staleness checks, not conversation
Most recent tool result (not yet submitted)
Model reasoned from them; editing creates inconsistency
Model's own words — altering them confuses future context
Incrementing skips work; decrementing can re-run side effects

from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Any

app = FastAPI()

class EditRequest(BaseModel):
    operation: str
    payload: dict[str, Any]
    reason: str = ""   # for audit log

@app.post("/agents/{agent_id}/checkpoints/{snapshot_id}/edit")
async def edit_snapshot(agent_id: str, snapshot_id: str, body: EditRequest):
    snapshot = await store.load(snapshot_id)
    if not snapshot or snapshot.agent_id != agent_id:
        raise HTTPException(404, "Snapshot not found")

    edit = StateEdit(operation=body.operation, payload=body.payload)
    try:
        updated = apply_edit(snapshot, edit)
    except (ValueError, KeyError) as e:
        raise HTTPException(400, str(e))

    # Save under the same snapshot_id (overwrite)
    await store.save(updated)

    # Audit the edit
    _log_state_edit(agent_id, snapshot_id, body.operation, body.reason)

    return {
        "snapshot_id": snapshot_id,
        "operation": body.operation,
        "queue_length": len(updated.work_queue),
        "message_count": len(updated.messages),
    }

def _log_state_edit(agent_id: str, snapshot_id: str, operation: str, reason: str):
    import json
    from pathlib import Path
    from datetime import datetime, UTC
    record = {
        "ts": datetime.now(UTC).isoformat(),
        "agent_id": agent_id,
        "snapshot_id": snapshot_id,
        "operation": operation,
        "reason": reason,
    }
    Path("state_edits.jsonl").open("a").write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
The most common edit is injecting a correction message — telling the agent it made an error or needs to change direction. The injection appears as a new user turn at the end of the message history:

Prefix injected corrections with [HUMAN CORRECTION] or a similar marker. This makes it visible in conversation logs that a human intervened, and it signals to the model that this is authoritative instruction, not just context.

If a tool returned incorrect data that the agent has not yet acted on, you can correct the result before it reaches the model. This is only safe for the most recent tool call — correcting historical results produces an inconsistent conversation:
For agents processing a list of items, a human may want to remove items that are no longer relevant, add new ones, or reorder priority:

# Remove a cancelled order from the processing queue
remove_edit = StateEdit(
    operation="queue_remove",
    payload={"item_id": "order-42"},
)

# Add a newly discovered high-priority item to the front
add_edit = StateEdit(
    operation="queue_add",
    payload={"item": {"id": "order-99", "priority": "high", "data": {...}}},
)

# Reorder so high-priority items come first
reorder_edit = StateEdit(
    operation="queue_reorder",
    payload={"order": ["order-99", "order-10", "order-11"]},
)

snap = apply_edit(snapshot, remove_edit)
snap = apply_edit(snap, add_edit)
snap = apply_edit(snap, reorder_edit)
await store.save(snap)
Before saving an edited snapshot, validate the resulting message list to ensure it maintains structural integrity:
Before applying edits, show operators a human-readable diff so they can confirm exactly what will change. This is especially important for message injections and tool result corrections:
Operators often want to apply several edits atomically — for example, inject a correction and also remove a now-invalid queue item. Apply them in sequence before saving:
Maintain an append-only edit history alongside each snapshot so operators can understand what interventions were made and when:

from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, UTC

EDIT_LOG = Path("agent_state_edits.jsonl")

def record_edit(
    agent_id: str,
    snapshot_id: str,
    operation: str,
    payload_summary: str,
    operator: str,
    reason: str,
):
    record = {
        "ts":             datetime.now(UTC).isoformat(),
        "agent_id":       agent_id,
        "snapshot_id":    snapshot_id,
        "operation":      operation,
        "payload_summary": payload_summary,
        "operator":       operator,
        "reason":         reason,
    }
    with EDIT_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

# Query: all edits for a given agent
def get_edit_history(agent_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    if not EDIT_LOG.exists():
        return []
    records = [json.loads(line) for line in EDIT_LOG.read_text().splitlines()]
    return [r for r in records if r["agent_id"] == agent_id]
Model sees its own words changed; reasoning breaks
Only inject new user messages; never mutate past assistant content
Correcting tool results the model already responded to
Retroactive inconsistency — model's reply no longer fits the result
Only correct the most recent pending tool result
Invalid role sequences cause API errors on next model call
Cannot reconstruct what a human changed during an incident
Always call record_edit() in the endpoint handler
Reorder fails when items lack id fields
Require id on every work-queue item at enqueue time

Editing agent state while paused lets humans correct the agent's direction, fix bad tool results, prune work queues, and adjust approval caches — all without discarding completed work. The key safety rule is to only edit forward-looking state (the work queue, new injected messages, the session-approved set) and to avoid rewriting historical assistant turns or tool results that the model has already reasoned from. Always validate the resulting message list before saving, and log every edit with a human-readable reason for the audit trail.
